author: Kent Bull
content: Reading Mind Map for KERI
pageTitle: Reading Mind Map: Key Event Receipt Infrastructure – KERI

author: Kent Bull
content: “Make things as simple as possible, but no simpler.” – Albert Einstein.
pageTitle: Reading Mind Map: Key Event Receipt Infrastructure – KERI

author: Kent Bull
content: In the spirit of keeping things simple for the new trust layer for the internet Dr. Samuel A Smith used the principle of “minimally sufficient means” to create Key Event Receipt Infrastructure, or KERI.
pageTitle: Reading Mind Map: Key Event Receipt Infrastructure – KERI

author: Kent Bull
content: Welcome to KERI, the world of scalable, decentralized, authentic data Allow me to give you a warm welcome to the world of Key Event Receipt Infrastructure, a special place in the self sovereign identity (SSI) ecosystem. Abbreviated as KERI, this flavor of decentralized key management infrastructure (DKMI) powers the main thrust of the movement for individual, personal control of data and privacy in the new private internet.
pageTitle: Reading Mind Map: Key Event Receipt Infrastructure – KERI

author: Kent Bull
content: Give me a map, please!
pageTitle: Reading Mind Map: Key Event Receipt Infrastructure – KERI

author: Kent Bull
content: Say you want to learn KERI, what is your first step? Reading the white paper, all 141 pages of it? As of today the answer is yes. Yet the KERI white paper is only the beginning, not the end.
pageTitle: Reading Mind Map: Key Event Receipt Infrastructure – KERI

author: Kent Bull
content: How can you come up to speed on all of the important IETF Draft Specifications around and important to KERI? Use the following pie graph and chart as a map:

author: Kent Bull
content: Guided Tour
pageTitle: Reading Mind Map: Key Event Receipt Infrastructure – KERI

author: Kent Bull
content: The KERI whitepaper, the KERI IETF Draft spec, and the did:keri spec make up 51% of the required reading yet read the whitepaper first and then come back to read the KERI IETF Draft spec at the end after all of the other reading since the KERI IETF Draft spec includes items from the ACDC and CESR specs.
pageTitle: Reading Mind Map: Key Event Receipt Infrastructure – KERI

author: Kent Bull
content: Then SAID and OOBI are the next good unit. SAID is a foundational concept for both the OOBI and ACDC specs. SAIDs are introduced in the KERI whitepaper though are important enough to have their own specification.
pageTitle: Reading Mind Map: Key Event Receipt Infrastructure – KERI

author: Kent Bull
content: ACDC, PTEL, and IPEX are good to do next. PTEL and IPEX directly depend on ACDC to make sense.
pageTitle: Reading Mind Map: Key Event Receipt Infrastructure – KERI

author: Kent Bull
content: Finish it off with CESR and CESR Proof Sigs, the text and binary communication protocol to make all of this high performance.
pageTitle: Reading Mind Map: Key Event Receipt Infrastructure – KERI

author: Kent Bull
content: KERIOX (Rust Implementation of KERI)

author: Kent Bull
content: Why so many pages?
pageTitle: Reading Mind Map: Key Event Receipt Infrastructure – KERI

author: Kent Bull
content: Print and select “Save as PDF” for each of the IETF Draft Specifications located at the table in the Web Of Trust KERI repository on GitHub as well as the KERI Whitepaper is where all the page counts located here came from. If you want to master KERI then roll up your sleeves and dig in. Whistle while you read!
pageTitle: Reading Mind Map: Key Event Receipt Infrastructure – KERI
